topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crimson 'D' slammed onto my desk, a physical weight that made my hands tremble. I stared at the paper, the ink blurring into meaningless shapes. My stomach clenched, a cold knot tightening in my chest. I replayed the lecture in my head, searching for the moment I had missed, the slip-up that had cost me this outcome. Every success I’d built up—the perfect attendance, the meticulous note-taking—felt suddenly fragile, like spun glass about to shatter. I immediately reached for my phone, my thumb hovering over my best friend’s contact. I needed to tell someone, to have them look at it, to find the flaw in the system, or maybe just to have someone else carry the weight of this disappointment for a moment. The silence of my room amplified the frantic thumping in my ears.

[story 2]

"This isn't fair," I muttered, the words barely escaping my lips as I crumpled the graded essay into a tight ball. The fluorescent lights of the library seemed to hum with mocking indifference. My shoulders slumped, the tension draining out of me until I felt strangely hollowed out. I walked to the empty chair across the table, not to study, but just to sit there, letting the sheer inadequacy of the score wash over me. I picked up my textbook again, but the words swam. I didn't know what to do next; the next step felt impossibly steep. I instinctively pulled out my planner, not to schedule the next assignment, but to check off the dates when I would inevitably need someone to explain the concepts again, to guide my hand through the labyrinth of the material. A sigh escaped me, heavy and defeated.

[story 3]

The number stared back at me, stark and unforgiving. My breath hitched. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to retreat, to find a safe harbor where mistakes didn't have consequences. My gaze drifted towards the door, as if expecting someone to walk in and simply take the paper from my numb fingers. I rubbed my temples, trying to force my focus back onto the complex equations, but my mind was a blank slate, filled only with the echo of failure. I started pacing the small space between my desk and the window, my movements jerky and uncoordinated. I needed reassurance, a steady hand to point out where I had gone astray. I picked up my phone, my fingers moving automatically to text my professor, already anticipating the carefully worded response that would validate my fears and somehow fix the situation.
